concepts.name: gauss-markov theorem
concepts.description: States that the least squares estimates of parameters have the smallest variance among all linear unbiased estimates.
concepts.quote: The Gauss–Markov theorem states that if we have any other linear estimator ˜θ = cT y that is unbiased for aT β, that is, E(cT y) = aT β, then Var(aT ˆβ) ≤Var(cT y).

concepts.name: hat matrix
concepts.description: The matrix H = X(XT X)^-1 XT that puts the hat on y by computing the orthogonal projection onto the column space of X.
concepts.quote: The matrix H = X(XT X)−1XT appearing in equation (3.7) is sometimes called the “hat” matrix because it puts the hat on y.

concepts.name: orthogonal projection
concepts.description: The projection of y onto the column space of X, minimizing the residual sum of squares.
concepts.quote: We minimize RSS(β) = ∥y −Xβ∥2 by choosing ˆβ so that the residual vector y −ˆy is orthogonal to this subspace. This orthogonality is expressed in (3.5), and the resulting estimate ˆy is hence the orthogonal projection of y onto this subspace.

concepts.name: qr decomposition
concepts.description: A decomposition X = QR where Q is orthogonal and R is upper triangular, providing a numerically stable basis for solving least squares.
concepts.quote: the so-called QR decomposition of X. Here Q is an N × (p + 1) orthogonal matrix, QT Q = I, and R is a (p + 1) × (p + 1) upper triangular matrix.

concepts.name: gram-schmidt orthogonalization
concepts.description: A procedure for constructing an orthogonal basis from a set of vectors by sequential orthogonalization.
concepts.quote: Algorithm 3.1 is known as the Gram–Schmidt procedure for multiple regression, and is also a useful numerical strategy for computing the estimates.

concepts.name: best subset selection
concepts.description: For each k, finds the subset of size k that gives the smallest residual sum of squares.
concepts.quote: Best subset regression ﬁnds for each k ∈{0, 1, 2, . . . , p} the subset of size k that gives smallest residual sum of squares (3.2).

concepts.name: leaps and bounds procedure
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm for best subset selection that makes it feasible for p as large as 30 or 40.
concepts.quote: An eﬃcient algorithm—the leaps and bounds procedure (Furnival and Wilson, 1974)—makes this feasible for p as large as 30 or 40.

concepts.name: forward stepwise selection
concepts.description: Starts with the intercept and sequentially adds the predictor that most improves the fit, producing a greedy nested sequence of models.
concepts.quote: Forward-stepwise selection starts with the intercept, and then sequentially adds into the model the predictor that most improves the ﬁt.

concepts.name: backward stepwise selection
concepts.description: Starts with the full model and sequentially deletes the predictor with the smallest Z-score (least impact on fit).
concepts.quote: Backward-stepwise selection starts with the full model, and sequentially deletes the predictor that has the least impact on the ﬁt. The candidate for dropping is the variable with the smallest Z-score

concepts.name: forward stagewise regression
concepts.description: Starts with all coefficients zero, identifies the variable most correlated with residual, computes simple linear regression coefficient of residual on that variable, and adds it to current coefficient without adjusting other variables.
concepts.quote: At each step the algorithm identiﬁes the variable most correlated with the current residual. It then computes the simple linear regression coeﬃcient of the residual on this chosen variable, and then adds it to the current coeﬃcient for that variable.

concepts.name: ridge regression
concepts.description: Shrinks regression coefficients by imposing an L2 penalty on their size, controlled by parameter lambda.
concepts.quote: Ridge regression shrinks the regression coeﬃcients by imposing a penalty on their size. The ridge coeﬃcients minimize a penalized residual sum of squares

concepts.name: weight decay
concepts.description: The neural network name for L2 regularization penalty on parameters.
concepts.quote: The idea of penalizing by the sum-of-squares of the parameters is also used in neural networks, where it is known as weight decay (Chapter 11).

concepts.name: singular value decomposition
concepts.description: A decomposition X = UDV^T where U and V are orthogonal matrices and D is diagonal, providing insight into variance structure of inputs.
concepts.quote: The singular value decomposition (SVD) of the centered input matrix X gives us some additional insight into the nature of ridge regression.

concepts.name: principal components
concepts.description: The columns of UD from SVD, representing orthogonal directions of maximum variance in the input space.
concepts.quote: The eigenvectors vj (columns of V) are also called the principal components (or Karhunen–Loeve) directions of X.

concepts.name: effective degrees of freedom
concepts.description: For ridge regression, df(lambda) = sum of d_j^2/(d_j^2 + lambda), measuring the number of parameters effectively used in a regularized fit.
concepts.quote: This monotone decreasing function of λ is the eﬀective degrees of freedom of the ridge regression ﬁt.

concepts.name: lasso
concepts.description: Shrinkage method using L1 penalty on coefficients, which can set some coefficients exactly to zero, performing continuous subset selection.
concepts.quote: Because of the nature of the constraint, making t suﬃciently small will cause some of the coeﬃcients to be exactly zero. Thus the lasso does a kind of continuous subset selection.

concepts.name: basis pursuit
concepts.description: The signal processing name for the lasso method.
concepts.quote: In the signal processing literature, the lasso is also known as basis pursuit (Chen et al., 1998).

concepts.name: soft thresholding
concepts.description: Lasso's coefficient shrinkage rule that translates each coefficient by a constant, truncating at zero.
concepts.quote: Lasso translates each coeﬃcient by a constant factor λ, truncating at zero. This is called “soft thresholding,” and is used in the context of wavelet-based smoothing

concepts.name: hard thresholding
concepts.description: Best subset selection's rule that drops all variables with coefficients smaller than the Mth largest.
concepts.quote: Best-subset selection drops all variables with coeﬃcients smaller than the Mth largest; this is a form of “hard-thresholding.”

concepts.name: elastic net penalty
concepts.description: A penalty combining L1 and L2 norms: alpha*beta^2 + (1-alpha)*|beta|, compromising between ridge and lasso.
concepts.quote: Zou and Hastie (2005) introduced the elastic-net penalty... a diﬀerent compromise between ridge and lasso.

concepts.name: least angle regression
concepts.description: A democratic version of forward stepwise that enters 'as much' of a predictor as it deserves, moving coefficients toward least squares continuously while keeping correlations tied.
concepts.quote: Least angle regression uses a similar strategy, but only enters “as much” of a predictor as it deserves. At the ﬁrst step it identiﬁes the variable most correlated with the response. Rather than ﬁt this variable completely, LAR moves the coeﬃcient of this variable continuously toward its least-squares value

concepts.name: lar-lasso connection
concepts.description: LAR with a simple modification produces the entire lasso path: drop variables when their coefficients hit zero.
concepts.quote: These observations lead to a simple modiﬁcation of the LAR algorithm that gives the entire lasso path, which is also piecewise-linear.

concepts.name: homotopy algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm for computing piecewise-linear lasso path discovered by Osborne et al.
concepts.quote: Osborne et al. (2000a) also discovered a piecewise-linear path for computing the lasso, which they called a homotopy algorithm.

concepts.name: degrees of freedom for adaptive fit
concepts.description: df(y-hat) = sum of Cov(y-hat_i, y_i) / sigma^2, measuring how hard a model fits the data.
concepts.quote: We deﬁne the degrees of freedom of the ﬁtted vector ˆy = (ˆy1, ˆy2, . . . , ˆyN) as df(ˆy) = sum Cov(ˆyi, yi)/σ2. This makes intuitive sense: the harder that we ﬁt to the data, the larger this covariance and hence df(ˆy).

concepts.name: principal components regression
concepts.description: Forms derived inputs as principal components of X, then regresses y on the first M components.
concepts.quote: Principal component regression forms the derived input columns zm = Xvm, and then regresses y on z1, z2, . . . , zM for some M ≤p.

concepts.name: partial least squares
concepts.description: Constructs linear combinations of inputs for regression using both X and y, weighting inputs by their univariate effect on y.
concepts.quote: Like principal component regression, partial least squares (PLS) is not scale invariant, so we assume that each xj is standardized to have mean 0 and variance 1. PLS begins by computing ˆϕ1j = ⟨xj, y⟩for each j.

concepts.name: canonical correlation analysis
concepts.description: Finds uncorrelated linear combinations of inputs and outputs with successively maximized correlations.
concepts.quote: Similar to PCA, CCA ﬁnds a sequence of uncorrelated linear combinations Xvm, m = 1, . . . , M of the xj, and a corresponding sequence of uncorrelated linear combinations Yum of the responses yk, such that the correlations are successively maximized.

concepts.name: reduced-rank regression
concepts.description: Regression method that constrains coefficient matrix to have low rank, borrowing strength among multiple responses via CCA truncation.
concepts.quote: Reduced-rank regression (Izenman, 1975; van der Merwe and Zidek, 1980) formalizes this approach in terms of a regression model that explicitly pools information.

concepts.name: curds and whey
concepts.description: A shrinkage method for multiple outputs that shrinks canonical variates between X and Y, a smooth version of reduced rank regression.
concepts.quote: Breiman and Friedman (1997) explored with some success shrinkage of the canonical variates between X and Y, a smooth version of reduced rank regression. Their proposal has the form... (the “c+w” stands for “Curds and Whey,” the name they gave to their procedure).

concepts.name: incremental forward stagewise regression
concepts.description: Forward stagewise with small step size epsilon, updating coefficient of most correlated variable by epsilon*sign(correlation) repeatedly.
concepts.quote: It generates a coeﬃcient proﬁle by repeatedly updating (by a small amount ϵ) the coeﬃcient of the variable most correlated with the current residuals.

concepts.name: infinitesimal forward stagewise regression
concepts.description: The limiting procedure as step size epsilon approaches zero in incremental forward stagewise, also called FS0 or L2boost.
concepts.quote: Letting ϵ →0 gives... We call this limiting procedure inﬁnitesimal forward stagewise regression or FS0.

concepts.name: l2boost
concepts.description: Another name for infinitesimal forward stagewise regression due to its connections to boosting.
concepts.quote: B¨uhlmann and Hothorn (2008) refer to the same procedure as “L2boost”, because of its connections to boosting.

concepts.name: dantzig selector
concepts.description: Minimizes L1 norm of coefficients subject to maximum absolute correlation of residuals with predictors being bounded.
concepts.quote: Candes and Tao (2007) proposed the following criterion: minimize ||β||1 subject to ||XT(y −Xβ)||∞≤s. They call the solution the Dantzig selector (DS).

concepts.name: grouped lasso
concepts.description: Lasso variant for pre-defined groups of predictors that encourages sparsity at both group and individual levels using group-wise L2 norms.
concepts.quote: The grouped-lasso minimizes the convex criterion [with] sqrt(p_ell) terms accounts for the varying group sizes, and Euclidean norm. Since the Euclidean norm of a vector βℓis zero only if all of its components are zero, this procedure encourages sparsity at both the group and individual levels.

concepts.name: relaxed lasso
concepts.description: Two-stage lasso: first run lasso to select predictors, then apply lasso again only to selected predictors with separate penalty parameter to reduce bias.
concepts.quote: Alternatively, one can use the lasso to select the set of non-zero predictors, and then apply the lasso again, but using only the selected predictors from the ﬁrst step. This is known as the relaxed lasso (Meinshausen, 2007).

concepts.name: scad penalty
concepts.description: Smoothly clipped absolute deviation penalty that shrinks large coefficients less severely than lasso to reduce bias.
concepts.quote: one can modify the lasso penalty function so that larger coeﬃcients are shrunken less severely; the smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) penalty of Fan and Li (2005) replaces λ|β| by Ja(β, λ)

concepts.name: adaptive lasso
concepts.description: Weighted lasso penalty using weights w_j = 1/|beta-hat_j|^nu from OLS, yielding consistent parameter estimates while maintaining convexity.
concepts.quote: The adaptive lasso (Zou, 2006) uses a weighted penalty of the form sum w_j|βj| where wj = 1/|ˆβj|ν, ˆβj is the ordinary least squares estimate and ν > 0... yields consistent estimates of the parameters while retaining the attractive convexity property of the lasso.

concepts.name: pathwise coordinate descent
concepts.description: Algorithm for computing lasso solution by fixing penalty parameter and optimizing successively over each parameter while holding others fixed.
concepts.quote: The idea is to ﬁx the penalty parameter λ in the Lagrangian form (3.52) and optimize successively over each parameter, holding the other parameters ﬁxed at their current values.

concepts.name: z-score for regression coefficients
concepts.description: The standardized coefficient z_j = beta-hat_j / se(beta-hat_j) used for hypothesis testing, distributed as t_{N-p-1} under null hypothesis.
concepts.quote: To test the hypothesis that a particular coeﬃcient βj = 0, we form the standardized coeﬃcient or Z-score z_j = ˆβj/sqrt(vjˆσ2) where vj is the jth diagonal element of (XT X)−1.

concepts.name: f-statistic for nested models
concepts.description: Test statistic for comparing nested linear models, measuring change in RSS per additional parameter normalized by error variance estimate.
concepts.quote: The F statistic measures the change in residual sum-of-squares per additional parameter in the bigger model, and it is normalized by an estimate of σ2.

concepts.name: variance-covariance matrix of least squares estimates
concepts.description: Var(beta-hat) = (X^T X)^{-1} sigma^2, giving the sampling variability of parameter estimates.
concepts.quote: The variance–covariance matrix of the least squares parameter estimates is easily derived from (3.6) and is given by Var(ˆβ) = (XT X)−1σ2.

concepts.name: multiple regression coefficient interpretation
concepts.description: The jth coefficient represents the additional contribution of x_j on y after x_j has been adjusted for all other predictors.
concepts.quote: The multiple regression coeﬃcient ˆβj represents the additional contribution of xj on y, after xj has been adjusted for x0, x1, . . . , xj−1, xj+1, . . . , xp.

concepts.name: multicollinearity
concepts.description: When predictors are highly correlated, coefficients become unstable with high variance, as small residual vectors lead to imprecise estimates.
concepts.quote: If xp is highly correlated with some of the other xk’s, the residual vector zp will be close to zero, and from (3.28) the coeﬃcient ˆβp will be very unstable.

concepts.name: bias-variance tradeoff in model selection
concepts.description: Shrinkage and selection methods sacrifice some bias to reduce variance, potentially improving overall prediction accuracy.
concepts.quote: Prediction accuracy can sometimes be improved by shrinking or setting some coeﬃcients to zero. By doing so we sacriﬁce a little bit of bias to reduce the variance of the predicted values, and hence may improve the overall prediction accuracy.

concepts.name: mean squared error decomposition
concepts.description: MSE = variance + (bias)^2, showing tradeoff between variance and bias in estimation.
concepts.quote: MSE(˜θ) = E[(˜θ −θ)2] = Var(˜θ) + [E(˜θ) −θ]2. The ﬁrst term is the variance, while the second term is the squared bias.

concepts.name: aic criterion
concepts.description: A criterion for model selection that accounts for the number of parameters fit, used to choose tuning parameters.
concepts.quote: cross-validation to estimate prediction error and select k; the AIC criterion is a popular alternative.

concepts.name: universal approximator
concepts.description: A class of models that can approximate any continuous function arbitrarily well (e.g., projection pursuit regression with enough terms).
concepts.quote: In fact, if M is taken arbitrarily large, for appropriate choice of gm the PPR model can approximate any continuous function in IRp arbitrarily well. Such a class of models is called a universal approximator.

concepts.name: l1 arc length
concepts.description: The total L1 distance traveled along a coefficient path, summing L1 norms of coefficient changes at each step.
concepts.quote: The L1 arc-length of a diﬀerentiable curve β(s) for s ∈[0, S] is given by TV(β, S) = integral || dot-beta(s)||1 ds. For the piecewise-linear LAR coeﬃcient proﬁle, this amounts to summing the L1 norms of the changes in coeﬃcients from step to step.

concepts.name: piecewise-linear solution path
concepts.description: Property of LAR and lasso where coefficient profiles are piecewise linear as tuning parameter varies, enabling efficient computation.
concepts.quote: By construction the coeﬃcients in LAR change in a piecewise linear fashion.

relationships.quote: The Gauss-Markov theorem implies that the least squares estimator has the smallest mean squared error of all linear estimators with no bias. However, there may well exist a biased estimator with smaller mean squared error.
The hat matrix H computes the orthogonal projection, and hence it is also known as a projection matrix.
Algorithm 3.1 is known as the Gram–Schmidt procedure for multiple regression... Introducing the diagonal matrix D with jth diagonal entry Djj = ∥zj∥, we get the so-called QR decomposition of X.
Forward-stepwise selection is a greedy algorithm, producing a nested sequence of models. In this sense it might seem sub-optimal compared to best-subset selection.
Backward selection can only be used when N > p, while forward stepwise can always be used.

Forward-stagewise regression (FS) is even more constrained than forward-stepwise regression... Unlike forward-stepwise regression, none of the other variables are adjusted when a term is added to the model.
The idea of penalizing by the sum-of-squares of the parameters is also used in neural networks, where it is known as weight decay
The singular value decomposition (SVD) of the centered input matrix X gives us some additional insight into the nature of ridge regression.
The SVD of the centered matrix X is another way of expressing the principal components of the variables in X.
Like linear regression, ridge regression computes the coordinates of y with respect to the orthonormal basis U. It then shrinks these coordinates by the factors d2j/(d2j + λ).
In the signal processing literature, the lasso is also known as basis pursuit
The lasso is a shrinkage method like ridge, with subtle but important differences... the L2 ridge penalty is replaced by the L1 lasso penalty
Lasso translates each coeﬃcient by a constant factor λ, truncating at zero. This is called “soft thresholding”
Best-subset selection drops all variables with coeﬃcients smaller than the Mth largest; this is a form of “hard-thresholding.”
Zou and Hastie (2005) introduced the elastic-net penalty... a diﬀerent compromise between ridge and lasso.
The elastic-net selects variables like the lasso, and shrinks together the coeﬃcients of correlated predictors like ridge.
Least angle regression (LAR) is a relative newcomer (Efron et al., 2004), and can be viewed as a kind of “democratic” version of forward stepwise regression
LAR is intimately connected with the lasso, and in fact provides an extremely eﬃcient algorithm for computing the entire lasso path
These observations lead to a simple modiﬁcation of the LAR algorithm that gives the entire lasso path
Osborne et al. (2000a) also discovered a piecewise-linear path for computing the lasso, which they called a homotopy algorithm.
Principal component regression forms the derived input columns zm = Xvm, and then regresses y on z1, z2, . . . , zM

We see that principal components regression is very similar to ridge regression: both operate via the principal components of the input matrix. Ridge regression shrinks the coeﬃcients of the principal components... principal components regression discards the p −M smallest eigenvalue components.
Like principal component regression, partial least squares (PLS) is not scale invariant... unlike principal components regression it uses y (in addition to X) for this construction.
Further analysis reveals that the variance aspect tends to dominate, and so partial least squares behaves much like ridge regression and principal components regression.
Similar to PCA, CCA ﬁnds a sequence of uncorrelated linear combinations... The leading canonical response variates are those linear combinations (derived responses) best predicted by the xj
one can show (Exercise 3.21) that the solution is given by a CCA of Y and X
Breiman and Friedman (1997) explored with some success shrinkage of the canonical variates between X and Y, a smooth version of reduced rank regression.
Consider the linear-regression version of the forward-stagewise boosting algorithm 16.1... It generates a coeﬃcient proﬁle by repeatedly updating (by a small amount ϵ) the coeﬃcient
Letting ϵ →0 gives the right panel of Figure 3.19... We call this limiting procedure inﬁnitesimal forward stagewise regression or FS0.
B¨uhlmann and Hothorn (2008) refer to the same procedure as “L2boost”, because of its connections to boosting.

Since FS0 is diﬀerent from the lasso, it is natural to ask if it optimizes a criterion... While the lasso makes optimal progress in terms of reducing the residual sum-of-squares per unit increase in L1-norm of the coeﬃcient vector β, FS0 is optimal per unit increase in L1 arc-length traveled along the coeﬃcient path.

In this form it resembles the lasso, replacing squared error loss by the maximum absolute value of its gradient... for smaller values of t, the DS procedure produces a diﬀerent path of solutions than the lasso.

The grouped lasso is one way to achieve this... Since the Euclidean norm of a vector βℓis zero only if all of its components are zero, this procedure encourages sparsity at both the group and individual levels.
one can use the lasso to select the set of non-zero predictors, and then apply the lasso again, but using only the selected predictors from the ﬁrst step. This is known as the relaxed lasso
one can modify the lasso penalty function so that larger coeﬃcients are shrunken less severely; the smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) penalty of Fan and Li (2005) replaces λ|β| by Ja(β, λ)
The adaptive lasso (Zou, 2006) uses a weighted penalty of the form sum w_j|βj|... This is a practical approximation to the |β|q penalties
An alternate approach to the LARS algorithm for computing the lasso solution is simple coordinate descent.
An alternate approach to the LARS algorithm for computing the lasso solution is simple coordinate descent.
By doing so we sacriﬁce a little bit of bias to reduce the variance of the predicted values... biased estimates such as ridge regression later in the chapter.
By doing so we sacriﬁce a little bit of bias to reduce the variance of the predicted values... The lasso falls somewhere between ridge regression and best subset regression
MSE(˜θ) = Var(˜θ) + [E(˜θ) −θ]2. The ﬁrst term is the variance, while the second term is the squared bias.
This monotone decreasing function of λ is the eﬀective degrees of freedom of the ridge regression ﬁt.

relationships.source: degrees of freedom for adaptive fit
relationships.quote: We need a more general deﬁnition for the eﬀective degrees of freedom of an adaptively ﬁtted model. We deﬁne the degrees of freedom of the ﬁtted vector

relationships.source: degrees of freedom for adaptive fit
relationships.quote: for the lasso, at any stage df(ˆy) approximately equals the number of predictors in the model.

relationships.source: degrees of freedom for adaptive fit
relationships.quote: For LAR and lasso, something magical happens... it can be shown that after the kth step of the LAR procedure, the eﬀective degrees of freedom of the ﬁt vector is exactly k.

When there are many correlated variables in a linear regression model, their coeﬃcients can become poorly determined and exhibit high variance... By imposing a size constraint on the coeﬃcients, as in (3.42), this problem is alleviated.
An eﬃcient algorithm—the leaps and bounds procedure (Furnival and Wilson, 1974)—makes this feasible for p as large as 30 or 40.
It can be shown (Exercise 3.1) that the zj in (3.12) are equivalent to the F statistic for dropping the single coeﬃcient βj from the model.
By construction the coeﬃcients in LAR change in a piecewise linear fashion.
These observations lead to a simple modiﬁcation of the LAR algorithm that gives the entire lasso path, which is also piecewise-linear.
For the piecewise-linear LAR coeﬃcient proﬁle, this amounts to summing the L1 norms of the changes in coeﬃcients from step to step.

While the lasso makes optimal progress in terms of reducing the residual sum-of-squares per unit increase in L1-norm of the coeﬃcient vector β, FS0 is optimal per unit increase in L1 arc-length traveled along the coeﬃcient path.
The question of how to choose k involves the tradeoﬀbetween bias and variance... cross-validation to estimate prediction error and select k; the AIC criterion is a popular alternative.
